Selecting Artiﬁcially-Generated Sentences for Fine-Tuning Neural Machine Translation

Alberto Poncelas and Andy Way ADAPT Centre, School of Computing, Dublin City University, Dublin, Ireland { firstname.lastname } @adaptcentre.ie

Abstract

Neural Machine Translation (NMT) models tend to achieve best performance when larger sets of parallel sentences are provided for trai- ning.

1f33f62d-b8b4-4eef-9a82-8f1460a48a2e.label: text_chunk
1f33f62d-b8b4-4eef-9a82-8f1460a48a2e.text: For this reason, augmenting the training set with artificially-generated sentence pairs can boost performance.Nonetheless, the performance can also be im- proved with a small number of sentences if they are in the same domain as the test set.

58dd07cc-f716-45b1-97ff-29c994dd865b.label: text_chunk
58dd07cc-f716-45b1-97ff-29c994dd865b.text: Accordingly, we want to explore the use of artificially-generated sentences along with data-selection algorithms to improve German- to-English NMT models trained solely with authentic data.In this work, we show how artificially- generated sentences can be more beneficial than authentic pairs, and demonstrate their ad- vantages when used in combination with data- selection algorithms.

a2c0d093-1b20-40f6-9d3d-ebfce8725674.label: text_chunk
a2c0d093-1b20-40f6-9d3d-ebfce8725674.text:
1 Introduction

The data used for training Machine Translation (MT) models consist mainly of a set of paral- lel sentences (a set of sentence-pairs in which each sentence is paired with its translation). As Neural Machine Translation (NMT) models typi- cally achieve best performance when using large sets of parallel sentences, they can benefit from the sentences created by Natural Language Ge- neration (NLG) systems.

118004d4-2458-4d4c-a21b-f8e9ba69fdeb.label: text_chunk
118004d4-2458-4d4c-a21b-f8e9ba69fdeb.text: Although artificial data is expected to be of lower quality than authentic sentences, it still can help the model to learn how to better generalize over the training instances and produce better translations.A popular technique used to create artificial data is the back-translation technique ( Sennrich et al. , 2016a ; Poncelas et al. , 2018c ).

32971fb4-78f4-4228-a862-540c50fc9c92.label: text_chunk
32971fb4-78f4-4228-a862-540c50fc9c92.text:
This consists of ge- nerating sentences in the source language by trans- lating monolingual sentences in the target langua- ge. Then, these sentences in both languages are
paired and can be used to augment the original par- allel training set used to build better NMT models.Nonetheless, if synthetic data are not in the sa- me domain as the test set, it can also hurt the per- formance.

22913299-1ae4-4b40-b54f-a6be89435efc.label: text_chunk
22913299-1ae4-4b40-b54f-a6be89435efc.text:
For this reason, we explore an alternati- ve approach to better use the artificially-generated training instances to improve NMT models. In par- ticular, we propose that instead of blindly adding back-translated sentences into the training set they can be considered as candidate sentences for a data-selection algorithm to decide which sentence- pairs should be used to fine-tune the NMT model.

 By doing that, instead of increasing the number of training instances in a motivated manner,

410845c4-3bce-423e-84a8-ea7c60032cdc.label: text_chunk
410845c4-3bce-423e-84a8-ea7c60032cdc.text: the ge- nerated sentences provide us with more chances of obtaining relevant parallel sentences (and still use smaller sets for fine-tuning).As we want to build task-specific NMT models, in this work we explore two data-selection algo- rithms that are classified as Transductive Algo- rithms (TA): Infrequent N -gram Recovery (INR) and Feature Decay Algorithms (FDA).

e73fc318-0336-4421-a018-27e1a6c26109.label: text_chunk
e73fc318-0336-4421-a018-27e1a6c26109.text: These me- thods use the test set S test (the document to be translated) as the seed to retrieve sentences. In transductive learning ( Vapnik , 1998 ) the goal is to identify the best training instances to learn how to classify a given test set. In order to select these sentences, the TAs search for those n -grams in the test set that are also present in the source side of the candidate sentences.

d78ce3ab-4caa-4242-88bb-49a25e3fb5de.label: text_chunk
d78ce3ab-4caa-4242-88bb-49a25e3fb5de.text: Although augmenting the candidate pool with more sentences should be beneficial, as the TAs select the sentences based on overlapping n -gram the mistakes produced by the model used for back- translation (which are those commonly addressed in NLG such as the generated word order or word choice) can be a disadvantage.In this work, we explore whether TAs are more inclined to select authentic or artificial sentences. In addition, we propose three different methods of

b03539e4-31a8-42ae-9442-a9cbe6475af4.label: text_chunk
b03539e4-31a8-42ae-9442-a9cbe6475af4.text:
how they can be combined into a single hybrid set. Finally, we investigate whether the hybrid sets re- trieved by TAs can be more useful than the authen- tic set of sentences to fine-tune NMT models.

2 Related Work

The work presented in this paper is based on two main concepts: the generation of synthetic sen- tences, and the selection of sentences from a set S of candidates.

20172c6d-e75a-4701-aaa2-3a0d3cae4bc2.label: text_chunk
20172c6d-e75a-4701-aaa2-3a0d3cae4bc2.text:
2.1 Use of Artiﬁcially-Generated Data to Improve MT Models

The proposal of Sennrich et al. ( 2016a ) showed that NMT models can be improved by back- translating a set of (monolingual) sentences in the target side into the source side using an MT model.

986c3387-bdf9-481f-8db6-3dbe0e13b3d6.label: text_chunk
986c3387-bdf9-481f-8db6-3dbe0e13b3d6.text:
Other uses of monolingual target-side sentences include building the parallel set by using a NULL token in the source side ( Sennrich et al.

 , 2016a ) or creating language models to improve the decoder ( G ¨ulc¸ehre et al. , 2015 ).Hoang et al. ( 2018 ) improve the model used for back-translation by training this model with incre- asing amounts of artificial sentences.

42c6f28a-2dc1-4ab5-b208-346760c7b1e8.label: text_chunk
42c6f28a-2dc1-4ab5-b208-346760c7b1e8.text:
They itera- tively improve the models creating artificial sen- tences of better quality.

Similarly to this paper, the use of artificially- generated sentences to fine-tuned models has also been explored by Chinea-Rios et al. ( 2017 ) whe- re they select monolingual authentic sentences in the source-side and translate them into the target language, or the work of Poncelas et al.

45699d19-8e58-482f-8501-68d0b5a4040b.label: text_chunk
45699d19-8e58-482f-8501-68d0b5a4040b.text:
( 2019a ) where they use back-translated sentences only to adapt the models.

2.2 Adaptation of NMT Models to the Test Set

The improvement of NMT models can be perfor- med by fine-tuning ( Luong and Manning , 2015 ; Freitag and Al-Onaizan , 2016 ), i.e. train the mo- dels for additional epochs using a small set of in-domain data.

72160e38-f33b-4366-b446-311681065d5b.label: text_chunk
72160e38-f33b-4366-b446-311681065d5b.text:
Alternatively, van der Wees et al. ( 2017 ) train models using smaller but more in- domain sentences in each epoch of the training process.

The use of the test set to retrieve relevant sen- tences for fine-tuning the model has been explo- red by Li et al. ( 2016 ), adapting a different model for each sentence in the test set, or Poncelas et al.

ad1d3b06-2756-4763-a8f7-2f9a2610921d.label: text_chunk
ad1d3b06-2756-4763-a8f7-2f9a2610921d.text:
( 2018b , 2019b ) where they adapt the model for the
complete test set using transductive data-selection algorithms.

3 Transductive Algorithms

In this paper, the sentences used to fine-tune the model are retrieved using INR and FDA. These methods select sentences by scoring each sentence s from the candidate pool S , and adding that with the highest score to a selected pool L .

4f9b2108-c451-4b36-a189-6bf09f95cf9d.label: text_chunk
4f9b2108-c451-4b36-a189-6bf09f95cf9d.text:
This pro- cess is performed iteratively until the selected pool contains N sentences.Infrequent n -gram Recovery (INR) ( Parcheta et al. , 2018 ; Gasc´o et al.

 , 2012 ): This method selects those sentences that contain n -grams from the test set that are infrequent (ignoring frequent words such as stop words or general-domain terms).

d1535e22-cd6b-482f-b70c-40fbed9ed1bc.label: text_chunk
d1535e22-cd6b-482f-b70c-40fbed9ed1bc.text:
A candidate sentence s ∈ S is scored according to the number of infre- quent n -grams shared with the set of sentences of the test set S test , computed as in ( 1 ):


score ( s ) = X ngr ∈{ S test T s } max (0 , t − C L ( ngr )) (1)
where t is the threshold that indicates the number of occurrences of an n -gram to be considered in- frequent.

0cd08f64-4cc7-4960-9d62-01b945845547.label: text_chunk
0cd08f64-4cc7-4960-9d62-01b945845547.text: If the number of occurrences of ngr in the selected pool ( C L ( ngr ) ) is above the threshold t , then the component max (0 , t − C S ( ngr )) is 0 and so the n -gram does not contribute to scoring the sentence.

d1fea890-bd38-4b91-82b2-bb34b18a6998.label: text_chunk
d1fea890-bd38-4b91-82b2-bb34b18a6998.text: Feature Decay Algorithms ( Bic¸ici and Yuret , 2011 ; Bic¸ici , 2013 ) also retrieve those sentences sharing the highest number of n -grams from the test set. However, in order to increase the variabi- lity and avoid selecting the same n -grams, those that have been selected are penalized is proportio- nal to the number of occurrences in L .

e61c8436-8f76-4f7d-a20f-9c4b9e858d7e.label: text_chunk
e61c8436-8f76-4f7d-a20f-9c4b9e858d7e.text:
The score of a sentence is computed as in ( 2 ):


score ( s, L ) = P ngr ∈ S test 0 . 5 C L ( ngr ) length ( s ) (2)
where length ( s ) indicates the number of words in the sentence s . According to the equation, the mo- re occurrences of ngr in L , the smaller the contri- bution is to the scoring of the sentence s .

9d7bb679-09d6-4d07-b58e-37beb18e1a62.label: text_chunk
9d7bb679-09d6-4d07-b58e-37beb18e1a62.text:
3.1 Models Adapted with Hybrid Data

In order to fine-tune models with hybrid data, we propose three methods of creating these sets: hybr , batch and online . These methods can be classified depending on whether the combination is perfor- med before or after the execution of the TA.Combine Before Selection. This approach con- sists of selecting from a hybrid set ( hybr ).

f8182a44-319c-4864-8626-49d87bcf624a.label: text_chunk
f8182a44-319c-4864-8626-49d87bcf624a.text: This in- volves concatenating both the authentic candidate S auth and artificial S synth sentences as a first step and then executing the TAs with the new candidate set S auth + synth .Combine After Selection. Another approach is to force the presence of both authentic and synthe- tic sentences by using different proportions of TA- selected authentic ( L auth ) and synthetic ( L synth ) sentence pairs.

a2edb7f4-96fa-4202-ad60-697d73c8689f.label: text_chunk
a2edb7f4-96fa-4202-ad60-697d73c8689f.text: We concatenate the top- ( N ∗ γ ) sentences from the selected authentic set and the top- ( N ∗ (1 − γ )) from the synthetic set. The value of γ ∈ [0 , 1] indicates the proportion of authentic and synthetic sentences. For example, γ = 0 . 75 indicates that the 75% of sentences in the dataset are authentic and the remaining 25% are artificial- ly generated.

91445b49-2066-4635-bb69-1c53976c4cd4.label: text_chunk
91445b49-2066-4635-bb69-1c53976c4cd4.text: The selected synthetic set L synth can be ob- tained by executing the TAs on artificial candi- date sentences S synth ( batch ). This implies that the sentences will be retrieved by finding overlaps of n -grams between the test set and artificial sen- tences.Alternatively, the retrieval may be carried by finding overlaps in the target-side ( online ) as they are human-produced sentences.

e3157226-42d5-4dbb-91a9-cad9eee32b8d.label: text_chunk
e3157226-42d5-4dbb-91a9-cad9eee32b8d.text: However, as the test set is in the source language, we need to first generate an approximated translation of the test with a general-domain MT model ( Poncelas et al. , 2018a , d ). Unlike in batch , the advantage of this approach is that it is not necessary to generate the source side of the whole set of monolingual sen- tences, but rather only those selected by the TA.

1187268d-a0ad-45dd-a5b8-bd4ebada8c0a.label: text_chunk
1187268d-a0ad-45dd-a5b8-bd4ebada8c0a.text:
4 Experiments

4.1 Data and Models Settings

We build German-to-English NMT models using the following datasets:

• Training data:

 German-English parallel sen- tences provided in WMT 2015 ( Bojar et al. , 2015 ) (4.5M sentence pairs).

c67f6afe-e3e8-4812-81b6-f69a367a4b91.label: text_chunk
c67f6afe-e3e8-4812-81b6-f69a367a4b91.text:
• Test sets: We evaluate the models with two test sets in different domains:

– BIO test set :

 the Cochrane 1 dataset from the WMT 2017 biomedical translation shared task ( Yepes et al. , 2017 ).

– NEWS test set : The test set provided in WMT 2015 News Translation Task.

All these data sets are tokenized, truecased, and Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) ( Sennrich et al.

5fd6bfdc-5c5e-4bf3-95d9-d91676600453.label: text_chunk
5fd6bfdc-5c5e-4bf3-95d9-d91676600453.text:
, 2016b ) is applied using 89,500 merge operations.The NMT models are built using the at- tentional encoder-decoder framework with OpenNMT-py 2 ( Klein et al. , 2017 ).

 We use the default values in the parameters: 2-layer LSTM ( Hochreiter and Schmidhuber , 1997 ) with 500 hidden units.

dbd97f0c-4c01-4420-b548-07ad895edd43.label: text_chunk
dbd97f0c-4c01-4420-b548-07ad895edd43.text:
The size of the vocabulary is 50,000 words for each language.

In order to retrieve sentences, we use the TAs with default configuration (using n -grams of or- der 3 to find overlaps between the seed and the training data) to extract sets of 100K, 200K, and 500K sentences. We use a threshold of t = 40 for INR although this causes the INR to retrieve less than 500K sentences.

21da138f-d9e4-42cc-b71e-3ad8e9b04215.label: text_chunk
21da138f-d9e4-42cc-b71e-3ad8e9b04215.text:
Accordingly, the results shown for INR will include only 100K and 200K sentences.

4.2 Back-Translation Generation Settings

In order to generate artificial sentences, we use an NMT model (we refer to it as BT model ) to back- translate sentences from the target language into the source language.

cf56a9cc-0617-4df0-8a52-b091e8976e4b.label: text_chunk
cf56a9cc-0617-4df0-8a52-b091e8976e4b.text:
This model is built by trai- ning a model with 1M sentences sampled from the training data and using the same configuration de- scribed above (but in the reverse language directi- on, English-to-German).

As we want to compare authentic and synthetic sentences, we back-translate the target-side of the training data using the BT model.

219e12ee-79f1-4e33-a792-941175a79a95.label: text_chunk
219e12ee-79f1-4e33-a792-941175a79a95.text:
By doing this we ensure both sets are comparable which allows us to perform a fair analysis of whether artificial sentences are more likely to be selected by a TA and which are more useful to fine-tune the models.Note also that there are 1M sentences that have been generated by translating the same target-side sentences used in training.

 This could cause the generated sentences to be exactly the same as au- thentic ones. However,

4397514c-3a34-4ae9-8519-679a17eb9664.label: text_chunk
4397514c-3a34-4ae9-8519-679a17eb9664.text: We have presented three methods of creating such hybrid data: (i) by allowing the TA decide whether to select authentic or synthetic data ( hy- br ); (ii) by performing independent executions of the TA on authentic and synthetic sets ( batch ); and (iii) using an MT-generated seed to select mono- lingual sentences so only the extracted subset is back-translated ( online ).

9adb3f0d-67a5-4b6c-bdcf-3a063f47aadd.label: text_chunk
9adb3f0d-67a5-4b6c-bdcf-3a063f47aadd.text: The experiments showed that artificially- generated sentences can be as competitive as au- thentic data, as models built with different propor- tions of authentic and synthetic data achieve si- milar or even better performance than those fine- tuned with authentic pairs only.

043221dc-f7f2-44f4-a2c8-bd6154cc9074.label: text_chunk
043221dc-f7f2-44f4-a2c8-bd6154cc9074.text: On one hand, tho- se sentences whose target-sides could hurt the per- formance of NMT (such as sentences in a diffe- rent language to that expected) causes the back- translated sentence to also contain unnatural n - grams and so TAs would not select them.

3f92220d-7bd1-4c34-810e-d6ea9006b523.label: text_chunk
3f92220d-7bd1-4c34-810e-d6ea9006b523.text:
On the other hand, if the source-side sentence is not an accurate translation of the target side (the problem of comparable corpus), the back-translated coun- terpart can be a better alternative to use as training data.In the future, we want to explore other language
pairs and other transductive algorithms. Another limitation of this work is that we have augmented the candidate pool with synthetic sentences gene- rated by a single model.

f0756ef0-e82b-460d-9e05-0e36369867bf.label: text_chunk
f0756ef0-e82b-460d-9e05-0e36369867bf.text: We propose to explore whether using several models for generating the synthetic sentences (including different approa- ches such as combining statistical and neural mo- del ( Poncelas et al. , 2019c )) to augment the can- didate pool can cause the selected data to further improve NMT models.

community_summary.155:
The relationships outlined in the knowledge graph highlight significant contributions by various researchers in the fields of natural language processing and machine learning. 

1. **Di Fabbrizio et al.**: They developed a **hybrid system** that integrates both extractive techniques and abstractive summaries, showcasing an innovative approach to summarization.

2. **Radev et al.**: This group is credited with the development of the **BERT CENT model**, which plays a role in the Salience-biased Extracts model, indicating its importance in enhancing summarization techniques.

3. **Denkowski and Lavie**: They are recognized for creating the **METEOR evaluation metric**, which is significant for assessing the quality of generated summaries, particularly in the context of the work by Wang and Ling.

4. **Diederik P. Kingma**: He is one of the developers of the **Adam optimization algorithm**, a widely adopted method in machine learning that improves the efficiency of training models.

5. **Chin-Yew Lin**: He developed the **ROUGE package**, a critical tool for evaluating the quality of summaries in natural language processing, further emphasizing the importance of evaluation metrics in this field.

Overall, these relationships illustrate the collaborative and innovative efforts of researchers in advancing techniques and tools for summarization and evaluation in natural language processing and machine learning.

community_summary.157:
The relationships outlined in the knowledge graph highlight the following key entities and their interconnections:

1. **Neural Network-Based Methods**: These methods incorporate an **Extractive Step** as a crucial component of their two-stage approach, which is designed to effectively process large sets of reviews. This relationship emphasizes the importance of the extractive step in enhancing the functionality of neural network-based techniques.

2. **HIT (Human Intelligence Task)**: Each HIT is characterized by the inclusion of a **Summary** that participants (turkers) are required to read as part of their task. This relationship underscores the role of summaries in facilitating the completion of HITs, ensuring that turkers have the necessary information to perform their tasks effectively.

In summary, the relationships illustrate how neural network-based methods leverage an extractive step for processing reviews and how HITs are structured around summaries that guide turkers in their tasks.
